God chose Abram and set him apart from his fellow idolaters so that he could reserve a people for himself, among whom true worship would be maintained until the coming of Christ. From then on, Abram and his descendants are almost the only subject of the history in the Bible. Abram was tested to see if he loved God more than all else and whether he would willingly leave everything to go with God. His relatives and his father's household were a constant temptation; he could not remain among them without risking being influenced by their ways. Those who abandon their sins and turn to God will find great benefit from the change. The command that God gave to Abram is similar to the gospel call, because natural affection must give way to Divine grace. Sin and all its opportunities must be forsaken, especially bad company. Here are many great and precious promises. All of God’s commands come with promises for those who obey them. 1. I will make you into a great nation. When God took Abram from his own people, he promised to make him the head of another people. 2. I will bless you. Believers who obey will surely inherit blessings. 3. I will make your name great. The names of obedient believers will certainly be made great. 4. You will be a blessing. Good men are a blessing to their country. 5. I will bless those who bless you and curse those who curse you. God will ensure that no one loses out by serving his people. 6. Through you all the families of the earth will be blessed. Jesus Christ is the great blessing of the world, the greatest the world has ever known. All the true blessings the world now possesses or ever will possess are due to Abram and his descendants. Through them we have a Bible, a Savior, and a gospel. They are the foundation on which the Christian church is built.

Abram believed that the blessing of the Almighty would compensate for all he could lose or leave behind, supply all his needs, and fulfill and surpass all his desires; and he knew that only misery would follow disobedience. Such believers, being justified by faith in Christ, have peace with God. They continue on their way to Canaan. They are not discouraged by the difficulties they encounter nor drawn aside by the pleasures they find. Those who set out for heaven must persevere until the end. What we undertake in obedience to God’s command and in humble reliance on his care will certainly succeed and end with comfort at last. Canaan was not, like other lands, merely an outward possession but a symbol of heaven, and in this respect the patriarchs so earnestly valued it.

Abram found the country populated by Canaanites, who were undesirable neighbors. He journeyed onward. Sometimes it is the lot of good men to be unsettled and often to move into various regions. Believers must view themselves as strangers and sojourners in this world. But observe how much comfort Abram had in God. When he could find little satisfaction in interacting with the Canaanites whom he found there, he experienced great pleasure in fellowship with that God who brought him there and did not abandon him. Fellowship with God is maintained through the word and prayer. God reveals himself and his favors to his people gradually; before, he had promised to show Abram this land, now, to give it to him: as grace grows, so does comfort. It seems Abram also understood it as a grant of a better land, of which this was a symbol; for he looked for a heavenly country. As soon as Abram arrived in Canaan, even though he was merely a stranger and sojourner there, he established and maintained the worship of God in his family. He not only observed the ceremonial aspects of religion, such as offering sacrifices, but also made it a priority to seek his God and call on his name; that spiritual sacrifice with which God is well pleased. He proclaimed concerning the name of the Lord; he taught his family and neighbors about the true God and his holy religion. The practice of family worship is a good tradition, not new, but an ancient custom of the saints. Abram was wealthy, had a large family, was now unsettled, and among enemies; yet wherever he pitched his tent, he built an altar: wherever we go, let us not fail to take our religion along with us.

There is no state on earth free from trials, nor any character free from flaws. There was famine in Canaan, the glory of all lands, and unbelief, with the evils it always brings, in Abram, the father of the faithful. Perfect happiness and perfect purity exist only in heaven. Abram, when he had to leave Canaan for a time, went to Egypt so that he would not appear to be looking back, and intending to stay there no longer than necessary. There Abram concealed his relationship to Sarai, equivocated, and instructed his wife and attendants to do the same. He withheld a truth, effectively denying it, and thereby exposed both his wife and the Egyptians to sin. The grace for which Abram was most known was faith; yet he fell through unbelief and distrust of Divine providence, even after God had appeared to him twice. Alas, what will become of weak faith when strong faith is shaken like this! If God did not deliver us, many times, from difficulties that we bring upon ourselves by our own sin and folly, we would be ruined. He does not deal with us according to our deserts. Chastisements are beneficial when they deter us from a sinful path and lead us to fulfill our duty, particularly the duty of restoring what we have wrongfully taken or kept. Pharaoh’s rebuke of Abram was very just: What is this that you have done? How unbecoming of a wise and good man! If those who profess religion do something unfair and deceptive, especially if they say something close to a lie, they should expect to hear about it; and they should be thankful for those who will tell them so. The sending away was kind. Pharaoh was far from having any design to kill Abram, as he feared, that he took particular care of him. We often worry needlessly. Many times we fear where no fear is. Pharaoh instructed his servants not to harm Abram in any way. It is not enough for those in authority that they do not harm themselves; they must keep their servants and those around them from doing harm.
